nativeRange.habitat: Shallow marine environments including inner reef flats, sheltered lagoons, and algal or silty reefs; also found among attached Sargassum on shallow rubble flats at depths of 1–15 m

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding primarily on filamentous green algae and to a lesser extent on fleshy and coralline red algae, as well as benthic invertebrates. In captivity tends to accept a varied diet of mysis shrimp, krill, chopped clams, and other meaty foods; algae-based foods and vegetable matter are generally advised to reflect the species' natural herbivorous leanings. Hard-shelled prey such as clams on the half shell or small snails are recommended periodically to help wear down the species' continuously growing beak-like fused teeth.

imageCandidates.notes: Rickard Zerpe; 2133x1600. Tack-sharp, vibrant in-situ shot of the whole fish against a dark background; clearly shows white belly, blue lines radiating from the orange eye, orange reticulate dorsal markings and the dark dorsal ocellus diagnostic of C. bennetti.
Philippe Bourjon (Reunion); 1200x911. Sharp side profile of whole fish over reef; olive-green dorsal, white belly, blue eye-lines, orange spotting and dark lateral band all clearly visible.

Diego Delso (Zanzibar); 1798x1199. Crisp in-water shot of whole fish on dark background; green dorsal, white/yellow belly, blue eye-lines and dark saddle; adds a different individual/angle for variety.

summary: Bennett's Sharpnose Puffer (Canthigaster bennetti) is a small Indo-West Pacific toby reaching around 10 cm, found on shallow reef flats and sheltered lagoons. Best kept singly in fish-only or FOWLR systems; tends to nip invertebrates and corals.
careNotes:
Bennett's Sharpnose Puffer (Canthigaster bennetti, Bleeker 1854) is a widely distributed member of the family Tetraodontidae — the true pufferfish — ranging across the Indo-West Pacific from East Africa south to Port Alfred, South Africa, and east to the Tuamotu Islands, with records north to southern Taiwan and south to New South Wales, Australia. A photo-documented population has also been noted in the Southeast Atlantic.

In the wild, this species tends to inhabit inner reef flats and sheltered lagoons, and is commonly found on algal or silty reefs and among attached Sargassum on shallow rubble flats at depths of 1–15 m. It is one of the smaller Canthigaster tobies, with a maximum total length reported at 10.0 cm. Coloration is typically brownish to olive dorsally with white or pale undersides and scattered blue-lined spots.

Its diet in the wild is predominantly herbivorous — primarily filamentous green algae — supplemented by benthic invertebrates including small crustaceans and molluscs. In captivity it has generally been reported to accept a mixed diet of algae-based foods and meaty items. The species' beak-like fused teeth grow continuously and benefit from periodic offerings of hard-shelled prey such as clams on the half shell or small snails.

The species is monogamous in the wild. Males are typically territorial and are best kept one per tank; conspecific aggression and aggression toward other Canthigaster tobies have been reported. Fin-nipping tendencies toward long-finned tankmates and consumption of small ornamental invertebrates are characteristic of the genus as a whole.

Like all members of the family Tetraodontidae, Bennett's Sharpnose Puffer contains tetrodotoxin in its skin, viscera, and gonads. Under severe stress or at death there is a small risk of toxin release into the aquarium water, which can harm sensitive tankmates. The species should be transported in a water-filled container rather than a net to prevent defensive inflation, as inhaling air can be harmful.
breedingNotes: Bennett's Sharpnose Puffer is oviparous and monogamous. Wild spawning in Canthigaster species typically involves male territory defence and demersal egg attachment in algal tufts. No captive-breeding protocol for C. bennetti is documented, and breeding in home aquariums is generally considered impractical.

primary.name: LiveAquaria
primary.notes: The LiveAquaria puffers collection was fetched but did not list Canthigaster bennetti. 22 puffer species were listed without C. bennetti appearing among them. No dedicated LiveAquaria product page for this species was locatable. LiveAquaria served as the designated primary source per the manifest; because no species-specific page was available, FishBase was elevated to primary verification source and other Canthigaster species pages on LiveAquaria were used for care parameter consensus.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Primary data source for species identification. Confirmed: scientific name Canthigaster bennetti (Bleeker, 1854); family Tetraodontidae; order Tetraodontiformes; max total length 10.0 cm; native range Indo-West Pacific (East Africa to Tuamotu Islands, Taiwan to New South Wales, Southeast Atlantic); preferred temperature 25.1–29.3°C (mean 28.4°C); depth 1–15 m; habitat inner reef flats and sheltered lagoons; diet primarily filamentous green algae supplemented by red algae and benthic invertebrates; trophic level 2.5; monogamous reproductive system; IUCN Least Concern. Salinity and pH data not provided by FishBase for this species — inferred from marine habitat designation and genus-level consensus.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Saddle Valentini Puffer (comparable Canthigaster)
additional.notes: Used for cross-reference on aquarium care parameters for the Canthigaster genus: pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.020–1.025, 30-gallon minimum tank, semi-aggressive temperament, intermediate care level, reef-safe with caution status. C. valentini is a sister species of similar maximum size (~10–11 cm) and overlapping range.

additional.name: LiveAquaria — Pearl Toby Puffer (comparable Canthigaster)
additional.notes: Additional Canthigaster genus cross-reference: pH 8.1–8.4, salinity SG 1.020–1.025, 30–50 gal minimum tank for a 10–12 cm Canthigaster. Confirmed intermediate care level and semi-aggressive temperament as genus-typical values.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Canthigaster bennetti
additional.notes: Confirms max size 10 cm, distribution in the Indo-Pacific, IUCN Least Concern status, and that the species 'occasionally makes its way into the aquarium trade.' Stub article; no aquarium husbandry parameters given.

fish.breedingNotes: No documented captive breeding protocol; wild reproductive system is oviparous and monogamous with demersal egg attachment in algal tufts.
